**Hindustani classical music** (also known as **North Indian classical music** or **[Shastriya](/wiki/Shastra "Shastra") [Sangeet](/wiki/Sangeet_(music) "Sangeet (music)")**) is the [classical music](/wiki/Indian_classical_music "Indian classical music") of the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent")'s northern regions. It is played on instruments like the [veena](/wiki/Veena "Veena"), [sitar](/wiki/Sitar "Sitar") and [sarod](/wiki/Sarod "Sarod"). It diverged in the 12th-century from [Carnatic music](/wiki/Carnatic_music "Carnatic music"), the classical tradition of [southern India](/wiki/South_India "South India"). While Carnatic music largely uses compositions written in [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit"), [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language"), [Telugu](/wiki/Telugu_language "Telugu language"), [Kannada](/wiki/Kannada "Kannada"), [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam"), Hindustani music largely uses compositions written in [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit"), [Hindustani](/wiki/Hindustani_language "Hindustani language") ([Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi")-[Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu")), [Braj](/wiki/Braj_Bhasha "Braj Bhasha"), [Awadhi](/wiki/Awadhi_language "Awadhi language"), [Bhojpuri](/wiki/Bhojpuri_language "Bhojpuri language"), [Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language"), [Rajasthani](/wiki/Rajasthani_languages "Rajasthani languages") and [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language") and even [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language"), [Lucknavi](/wiki/Lucknavi "Lucknavi")
and, [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language"), etc.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Knowledge of Hindustani classical music is taught through a network of classical music schools, called *[gharana](/wiki/Gharana "Gharana")*. Hindustani classical music is an integral part of the [culture of North India](/wiki/North_Indian_culture "North Indian culture") and is performed across the country and internationally. Exponents of Hindustani classical music, including [Ustad](/wiki/Ustad "Ustad") [Bismillah Khan](/wiki/Bismillah_Khan "Bismillah Khan"), [Pandit](/wiki/Pandit "Pandit") [Bhimsen Joshi](/wiki/Bhimsen_Joshi "Bhimsen Joshi") and [Ravi Shankar](/wiki/Ravi_Shankar "Ravi Shankar") have been awarded the [Bharat Ratna](/wiki/Bharat_Ratna "Bharat Ratna"), the highest civilian award of India, for their contributions to the arts.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hindustani_classical_music&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Around the 12th-century, Hindustani classical music diverged from what eventually came to be identified as [Carnatic classical music](/wiki/Carnatic_classical_music "Carnatic classical music"). The central notion in both systems is that of a melodic [musical mode](/wiki/Musical_mode "Musical mode") or *[raga](/wiki/Raga "Raga")*, sung to a rhythmic cycle or *[tala](/wiki/Tala_(music) "Tala (music)")*. It is melodic music, with no concept of harmony. These principles were refined in the musical treatises *[Natya Shastra](/wiki/Natya_Shastra "Natya Shastra")*, by [Bharata](/wiki/Bharata_Muni "Bharata Muni") (2nd–3rd century CE), and *[Dattilam](/wiki/Dattilam "Dattilam")* (probably 3rd–4th century CE).[[3]](#cite_note-3)

In medieval times, the melodic systems were fused with ideas from Persian music, particularly through the influence of Sufi composers like [Amir Khusro](/wiki/Amir_Khusro "Amir Khusro"), and later in the [Mughal](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire") courts, noted composers such as [Tansen](/wiki/Tansen "Tansen") flourished, along with religious groups like the [Vaishnavites](/wiki/Vaishnavite "Vaishnavite"). Artists such as [Dalptaram](/wiki/Dalpatram "Dalpatram"), [Mirabai](/wiki/Mirabai "Mirabai"), [Brahmanand Swami](/wiki/Brahmanand_Swami "Brahmanand Swami") and [Premanand Swami](/wiki/Premanand_Swami "Premanand Swami") revitalized classical Hindustani music in the 16-18th century.

After the 16th century, the singing styles diversified into different [gharanas](/wiki/Gharana "Gharana") patronized in different princely courts. Around 1900, [Vishnu Narayan Bhatkhande](/wiki/Vishnu_Narayan_Bhatkhande "Vishnu Narayan Bhatkhande") consolidated the musical structures of Hindustani classical music, called [ragas](/wiki/Raga "Raga"), into a few [thaats](/wiki/Thaat "Thaat") based on their notes. This is a very flawed system but is somewhat useful as a heuristic.

Distinguished musicians who are [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") may be addressed as *[Pandit](/wiki/Pandit "Pandit")* and those who are [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") as *[Ustad](/wiki/Ustad "Ustad")*. An aspect of Hindustani music going back to [Sufi](/wiki/Sufi "Sufi") times is the tradition of religious neutrality: Muslim ustads may sing compositions in praise of Hindu deities, and Hindu pandits may sing similar Islamic compositions.

[Vishnu Digambar Paluskar](/wiki/Vishnu_Digambar_Paluskar "Vishnu Digambar Paluskar") in 1901 founded the Gandharva Mahavidyalaya, a school to impart formal training in Hindustani classical music with some historical Indian Music. This was a school open to all and one of the first in India to run on public support and donations, rather than royal patronage. Many students from the School's early batches became respected [musicians](/wiki/Musician "Musician") and teachers in North India. This brought respect to musicians, who were treated with disdain earlier. This also helped spread of Hindustani classical music to the masses from royal courts.

### Sanskritic tradition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hindustani_classical_music&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Sanskritic tradition")]

[Ravana](/wiki/Ravana "Ravana") and [Narada](/wiki/Narada "Narada") from Hindu tradition are accomplished musicians; [Saraswati](/wiki/Saraswati "Saraswati") with her [veena](/wiki/Veena "Veena") is the goddess of music. [Gandharvas](/wiki/Gandharva "Gandharva") are presented as spirits who are musical masters, and the gandharva style looks to music primarily for pleasure, accompanied by the [soma](/wiki/Soma_(drink) "Soma (drink)") rasa. In the *[Vishnudharmottara Purana](/wiki/Vishnudharmottara_Purana "Vishnudharmottara Purana")*, the [Naga](/wiki/N%C4%81ga "Nāga") king Ashvatara asks to know the [swaras](/wiki/Swara "Swara") from Saraswati.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

While the term *raga* is articulated in the *[Natya Shastra](/wiki/Natya_Shastra "Natya Shastra")* (where its meaning is more literal, meaning "color" or "mood"), it finds a clearer expression in what is called *Jati* in the *[Dattilam](/wiki/Dattilam "Dattilam")*, a text composed shortly after or around the same time as *Natya Shastra*. The Dattilam is focused on Gandharva music and discusses scales ([swara](/wiki/Swara "Swara")), defining a tonal framework called *grama* in terms of 22 micro-tonal intervals ([shruti](/wiki/Shruti "Shruti"))[[4]](#cite_note-4) comprising one octave. It also discusses various arrangements of the notes (*Murchhana*), the permutations and combinations of note-sequences (*tanas*), and *[alankara](/wiki/Alank%C4%81ra "Alankāra")* or elaboration. Dattilam categorizes melodic structure into 18 groups called *Jati*, which are the fundamental melodic structures similar to the [raga](/wiki/Raga "Raga"). The names of the Jatis reflect regional origins, for example *Andhri* and *Oudichya*[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*].

Music also finds mention in a number of texts from the [Gupta period](/wiki/Gupta_period "Gupta period"); [Kalidasa](/wiki/Kalidasa "Kalidasa") mentions several kinds of veena (parivadini, [vipanchi](/wiki/Vipanchi_veena "Vipanchi veena")), as well as percussion instruments ([mridang](/wiki/Mridang "Mridang")), the flute (*vamshi*) and conch (*[shankha](/wiki/Shankha "Shankha")*). Music also finds mention in [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") and [Jain](/wiki/Jain "Jain") texts from the earliest periods of the common era.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Narada's *[Sangita Makarandha](/wiki/Sangita_Makarandha "Sangita Makarandha")* treatise, from about 1100 CE, is the earliest text where rules similar to those of current Hindustani classical music can be found. Narada actually names and classifies the system in its earlier form before the Persian influences introduced changes in the system. [Jayadeva](/wiki/Jayadeva "Jayadeva")'s *[Gita Govinda](/wiki/Gita_Govinda "Gita Govinda")* from the 12th century was perhaps the earliest musical composition sung in the classical tradition called [Ashtapadi](/wiki/Ashtapadi "Ashtapadi") music.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the 13th century, [Sharangadeva](/wiki/%C5%9A%C4%81r%E1%B9%85gadeva "Śārṅgadeva") composed the *[Sangita Ratnakara](/wiki/Sangita_Ratnakara "Sangita Ratnakara")*, which has names such as the *Turushka Todi* ("Turkish [Todi](/wiki/Todi_(thaat) "Todi (thaat)")"), revealing an influx of ideas from Islamic culture. This text is the last to be mentioned by both the [Carnatic](/wiki/Carnatic_music "Carnatic music") and the Hindustani traditions and is often thought to date the divergence between the two.

### During the Delhi Sultanate and Mughal era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hindustani_classical_music&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: During the Delhi Sultanate and Mughal era")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Barbed_rubab_%28lute%29%2C_1660-1670_CE%2C_Golkonda.png/250px-Barbed_rubab_%28lute%29%2C_1660-1670_CE%2C_Golkonda.png)](/wiki/File:Barbed_rubab_(lute),_1660-1670_CE,_Golkonda.png)

[Golkonda](/wiki/Qutb_Shahi_dynasty "Qutb Shahi dynasty") 1660–1670. Musician plays a form of [rubab](/wiki/Rubab_(instrument) "Rubab (instrument)"). Related instruments, the Indian [sarod](/wiki/Sarod "Sarod"), [sursingar](/wiki/Sursingar "Sursingar") and [kamaica](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/kamaica "de:kamaica"), the Nepali-Tibetan-Bhutanese [tungana](/wiki/Tungana "Tungana"), the [Pamiri rubab](/wiki/Pamiri_rubab "Pamiri rubab") and the Uyghur [rawap](/wiki/Rawap "Rawap"). The family of instruments blended Persian and Indian cultures, and has been played by Hindus, Buddhists and Muslims.

The advent of Islamic rule under the [Delhi Sultanate](/wiki/Delhi_Sultanate "Delhi Sultanate") and later the [Mughal Empire](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire") over [northern India](/wiki/North_India "North India") caused considerable cultural interchange. Increasingly, musicians received patronage in the courts of the new rulers, who, in turn, started taking an increasing interest in local musical forms. While the initial generations may have been rooted in cultural traditions outside India, they gradually adopted many aspects from the Hindu culture from their kingdoms. This helped spur the fusion of Hindu and Muslim ideas to bring forth new forms of musical synthesis like [qawwali](/wiki/Qawwali "Qawwali") and [khyal](/wiki/Khyal "Khyal").

The most influential musician of the Delhi Sultanate period was [Amir Khusrau](/wiki/Amir_Khusrau "Amir Khusrau") (1253–1325), a composer in [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language"), [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language") and [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic"), as well as [Braj Bhasha](/wiki/Braj_Bhasha "Braj Bhasha"). He is credited with systematizing some aspects of Hindustani music and also introducing several ragas such as [Yaman Kalyan](/wiki/Yaman_Kalyan "Yaman Kalyan"), [Zeelaf](/wiki/Zeelaf "Zeelaf") and [Sarpada](/wiki/Sarpada "Sarpada"). He created six genres of music: khyal, [tarana](/wiki/Tarana "Tarana"), Naqsh, Gul, Qaul and Qalbana. A number of instruments (such as the [sitar](/wiki/Sitar "Sitar")) also developed in this time.

Amir Khusrau is sometimes credited with the origins of the khyal form, but the record of his compositions does not appear to support this. The compositions by the court musician [Sadarang](/wiki/Sadarang "Sadarang") in the court of [Muhammad Shah](/wiki/Muhammad_Shah "Muhammad Shah") bear a closer affinity to the modern khyal. They suggest that while khyal already existed in some form, Sadarang may have been the father of modern khyal.

Much of the musical forms innovated by these pioneers merged with the Hindu tradition, composed in the popular language of the people (as opposed to Sanskrit) in the work of composers like [Kabir](/wiki/Kabir "Kabir") or [Nanak](/wiki/Nanak "Nanak"). This can be seen as part of a larger [Bhakti](/wiki/Bhakti "Bhakti") tradition (strongly related to the [Vaishnavite](/wiki/Vaishnavite "Vaishnavite") movement) which remained influential across several centuries; notable figures include [Jayadeva](/wiki/Jayadeva "Jayadeva") (12th century), [Vidyapati](/wiki/Vidyapati "Vidyapati") (fl. 1375 CE), [Chandidas](/wiki/Chandidas "Chandidas") (14th–15th century), and [Meerabai](/wiki/Meerabai "Meerabai") (1555–1603 CE).

As the Mughal Empire came into closer contact with Hindus, especially under Jalal ud-Din [Akbar](/wiki/Akbar "Akbar"), music and dance also flourished. In particular, the musician [Tansen](/wiki/Tansen "Tansen") introduced a number of innovations, including ragas and particular compositions. Legend has it that upon his rendition of a nighttime raga in the morning, the entire city fell under a hush and clouds gathered in the sky so that he could light fires by singing the raga "Deepak".

At the royal house of [Gwalior](/wiki/Gwalior "Gwalior"), [Raja Mansingh Tomar](/wiki/Man_Singh_Tomar "Man Singh Tomar") (1486–1516 CE) also participated in the shift from Sanskrit to the local idiom ([Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi")) as the language for classical songs. He himself penned several volumes of compositions on religious and secular themes and was also responsible for the major compilation, the *Mankutuhal* ("Book of Curiosity"), which outlined the major forms of music prevalent at the time. In particular, the musical form known as [dhrupad](/wiki/Dhrupad "Dhrupad") saw considerable development in his court and remained a strong point of the [Gwalior gharana](/wiki/Gwalior_gharana "Gwalior gharana") for many centuries.

After the dissolution of the Mughal empire, the patronage of music continued in smaller princely kingdoms like [Awadh](/wiki/Awadh "Awadh"), [Patiala](/wiki/Patiala_State "Patiala State"), and [Banaras](/wiki/Benares_State "Benares State"), giving rise to the diversity of styles that is today known as [gharanas](/wiki/Gharana "Gharana"). Many musician families obtained large grants of land which made them self-sufficient, at least for a few generations (e.g. the [Sham Chaurasia](/wiki/Sham_Chaurasia "Sham Chaurasia") gharana). Meanwhile, the Bhakti and [Sufi](/wiki/Sufi "Sufi") traditions continued to develop and interact with the different gharanas and groups.

### Modern era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hindustani_classical_music&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Modern era")]

Until the late 19th century, Hindustani classical music was imparted on a one-on-one basis through the [guru-shishya](/wiki/Guru%E2%80%93shishya_tradition "Guru–shishya tradition") ("mentor-protégé") tradition. This system had many benefits but also several drawbacks. In many cases, the shishya had to spend most of his time, serving his guru with the hope that the guru might teach him a "[cheez](/wiki/Cheez_(music) "Cheez (music)")" (piece or nuance) or two. In addition, the system forced the music to be limited to a small subsection of the Indian community. To a large extent, it was limited to the palaces and dance halls. It was shunned by the intellectuals, avoided by the educated middle class, and in general, looked down upon as a frivolous practice.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

First, as the power of the [maharajahs](/wiki/Maharajah "Maharajah") and [nawabs](/wiki/Nawab "Nawab") declined in the early 20th century, so did their patronage. With the expulsion of [Wajid Ali Shah](/wiki/Wajid_Ali_Shah "Wajid Ali Shah") to [Calcutta](/wiki/Calcutta "Calcutta") after 1857, the Lucknavi musical tradition came to influence the music of the renaissance in [Bengal](/wiki/Bengal "Bengal"), giving rise to the tradition of *Ragpradhan gan* around the turn of the century. [Raja Chakradhar Singh of Raigarh](/wiki/Chakradhar_Singh "Chakradhar Singh") was the last of the modern-era Maharajas to patronize Hindustani classical musicians, singers and dancers.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Also, at the turn of the century, [Vishnu Digambar Paluskar](/wiki/Vishnu_Digambar_Paluskar "Vishnu Digambar Paluskar") and [Vishnu Narayan Bhatkhande](/wiki/Vishnu_Narayan_Bhatkhande "Vishnu Narayan Bhatkhande") spread Hindustani classical music to the masses in general by organizing music conferences, starting schools, teaching music in classrooms, devising a standardized grading and testing system, and standardizing the notation system.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Vishnu Digambar Paluskar emerged as a talented musician and organizer despite being blind from age of 12. His books on music, as well as the *[Gandharva Mahavidyalaya](/wiki/Gandharva_Mahavidyalaya "Gandharva Mahavidyalaya")* music school that he opened in [Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore") in 1901, helped foster a movement away from the closed gharana system.

Paluskar's contemporary (and occasional rival) Vishnu Narayan Bhatkhande recognized the many rifts that had appeared in the structure of Indian classical music. He undertook extensive research visits to a large number of gharanas, Hindustani as well as Carnatic, collecting and comparing compositions. Between 1909 and 1932, he produced the monumental four-volume work *Hindustani Sangeeta Paddhati*,[[9]](#cite_note-9) which suggested a transcription of Indian music, and described the many traditions in this notation. Finally, it suggested a possible categorization of [ragas](/wiki/Raga "Raga") based on their notes into a number of [thaats](/wiki/Thaat "Thaat") (modes), subsequent to the [Melakarta](/wiki/Melakarta "Melakarta") system that reorganized Carnatic tradition in the 17th century. The ragas that exist today were categorized according to this scheme, although there are some inconsistencies and ambiguities in Bhatkande's system.

In modern times, the government-run [All India Radio](/wiki/All_India_Radio "All India Radio"), [Bangladesh Betar](/wiki/Bangladesh_Betar "Bangladesh Betar") and [Radio Pakistan](/wiki/Radio_Pakistan "Radio Pakistan") helped bring the artists to public attention, countering the loss of the patronage system. The first star was [Gauhar Jan](/wiki/Gauhar_Jan "Gauhar Jan"), whose career was born out of [Fred Gaisberg](/wiki/Fred_Gaisberg "Fred Gaisberg")'s first recordings of Indian music in 1902. With the advance of films and other public media, musicians started to make their living through public performances. A number of [Gurukuls](/wiki/Gurukul "Gurukul"), such as that of [Alauddin Khan](/wiki/Alauddin_Khan "Alauddin Khan") at [Maihar](/wiki/Maihar "Maihar"), flourished. In more modern times, corporate support has also been forthcoming, as at the [ITC Sangeet Research Academy](/wiki/ITC_Sangeet_Research_Academy "ITC Sangeet Research Academy"). Meanwhile, Hindustani classical music has become popular across the world through the influence of artists such as [Ravi Shankar](/wiki/Ravi_Shankar "Ravi Shankar"), [Ali Akbar Khan](/wiki/Ali_Akbar_Khan "Ali Akbar Khan") and [Vikash Maharaj](/wiki/Vikash_Maharaj "Vikash Maharaj").

## Characteristics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hindustani_classical_music&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Characteristics")]

Indian classical music has seven basic notes with five interspersed half-notes, resulting in a 12-note scale. Unlike the 12-note scale in [Western music](/wiki/Western_music_(North_America) "Western music (North America)"), the base frequency of the scale is not fixed, and intertonal gaps ([temperament](/wiki/Interval_(music) "Interval (music)")) may also vary. The performance is set to a melodic pattern called a *raga* characterized in part by specific ascent ([aroha](/wiki/Arohana "Arohana")) and descent ([avaroha](/wiki/Avaroha "Avaroha")) sequences, "king" ([vadi](/wiki/Vadi_(Hindustani_classical_music) "Vadi (Hindustani classical music)")) and "queen" ([samavadi](/wiki/Samavadi "Samavadi")) notes and characteristic phrases ([pakad](/wiki/Pakad "Pakad")).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Ragas may originate from any source, including religious hymns, folk tunes, and music from outside the Indian subcontinent[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. For example, raga Khamaj and its variants have been classicized from folk music, while ragas such as Hijaz (also called Basant Mukhari) originated in Persian maqams.

## Principles of Hindustani music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hindustani_classical_music&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Principles of Hindustani music")]

The [Gandharva Veda](/wiki/Gandharva_Veda "Gandharva Veda") is a Sanskrit scripture describing the theory of music and its applications in not just musical form and systems but also in physics, medicine and magic.[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10) It is said that there are two types of sound: *āhata* (struck/audible) and *anāhata* (unstruck/inaudible).[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10) The inaudible sound is said to be the principle of all manifestation, the basis of all existence.[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10)

There are three main 'Saptak' which resemble to the 'Octaves' in Western Music except they characterize a total of seven natural (shuddha) notes or 'swaras' instead of eight. These are -- low (*mandra*), medium (*madhya)* and high (*tāra*).[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10) Each octave resonates with a certain part of the body, low octave in the heart, medium octave in the throat and high octave in the head. Each octave in total contains 12 different notes, containing 7 natural or 'shuddha' notes (S R G M P D N), 4 flat or 'komal' notes (R G D N), and 1 sharp or 'teevra' note (M).[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10)

The rhythmic organization is based on rhythmic patterns called [tala](/wiki/Tala_(music) "Tala (music)"). The melodic foundations are called ragas. One possible classification of ragas is into "melodic modes" or "parent scales," known as [thaats](/wiki/Thaat "Thaat"), under which most [ragas](/wiki/Raga "Raga") can be classified based on the notes they use.

Thaats may consist of up to seven scale degrees, or [swara](/wiki/Swara "Swara"). Hindustani musicians name these pitches using a system called [Sargam](/wiki/Swara "Swara"), the equivalent of the Western movable *do* [solfege](/wiki/Solfege "Solfege"):

* Sa ([ṣaḍja](/wiki/%E1%B9%A2a%E1%B8%8Dja "Ṣaḍja") षड्ज) = Do
* Re ([Rishabh](/wiki/Rishabh "Rishabh") ऋषभ) = Re
* Ga ([Gāndhāra](/wiki/G%C4%81ndh%C4%81ra "Gāndhāra") गान्धार) = Mi
* Ma ([Madhyam](/wiki/Madhyam "Madhyam") मध्यम) = Fa
* Pa ([Pancham](/wiki/Pancham_(svara) "Pancham (svara)") पञ्चम) = So
* Dha ([Dhaivat](/wiki/Dhaivat "Dhaivat") धैवत) = La
* Ni ([Nishād](/wiki/Nishad_(svara) "Nishad (svara)") निषाद) = Ti
* Sa ([ṣaḍja](/wiki/Shadja "Shadja") षड्ज) = Do

Both systems repeat at the octave. The difference between sargam and solfege is that re, ga, ma, dha, and ni can refer to either "Natural" (*shuddha*) or altered "Flat" (*komal*) or "Sharp" (*teevra*) versions of their respective scale degrees. As with movable do solfege, the notes are heard relative to an arbitrary tonic that varies from performance to performance, rather than to fixed frequencies, as on a xylophone. The fine intonational differences between different instances of the same swara are called [srutis](/wiki/Sruti_(music) "Sruti (music)"). The three primary registers of Indian classical music are mandra (lower), madhya (middle) and taar (upper). Since the octave location is not fixed, it is also possible to use provenances in mid-register (such as mandra-madhya or madhya-taar) for certain ragas. A typical rendition of Hindustani raga involves two stages:

* Alap: a rhythmically free improvisation on the rules for the raga in order to give life to the raga and flesh out its characteristics. The alap is followed by a long slow-tempo improvisation in vocal music, or by the jod and jhala in instrumental music.

Tans are of several types like Shuddha, Koot, Mishra, Vakra, Sapaat, Saral, Chhoot, Halaq, Jabda, Murki.

* *[Bandish](/wiki/Bandish "Bandish")* or *[Gat](/wiki/Bandish "Bandish")*: a fixed, melodic composition set in a specific raga, performed with rhythmic accompaniment by a [tabla](/wiki/Tabla "Tabla") or pakhavaj. There are different ways of systematizing the parts of a composition. For example:
  + [Sthaayi](/wiki/Sthayi "Sthayi"): The initial, [rondo](/wiki/Rondo "Rondo") phrase or line of a fixed, melodic composition
  + [Antara](/wiki/Antara_(music) "Antara (music)"): The first body phrase or line of a fixed, melodic composition. Explores the upper octave of a Raag. In Khayal compositions, this is sometimes where the poet's name can be found.
  + [Sanchaari](/w/index.php?title=Sanchaari&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sanchaari (page does not exist)"): The third body phrase or line of a fixed, melodic composition, seen more typically in [dhrupad bandishes](/wiki/Dhrupad "Dhrupad"). Usually explores the lower section of a given Raag.
  + [Aabhog](/wiki/Aabhog "Aabhog"): The fourth and concluding body phrase or line of a fixed, melodic composition, seen more typically in [Dhrupad bandishes](/wiki/Dhrupad "Dhrupad"). Continues to explore the upper octave of a Raag just like an Antara, but with more expansive phrases. This is often where the poet's name resides as a signature for Dhrupad compositions.
* There are three variations of bandish, regarding tempo:
  + *[Vilambit](/wiki/Vilambit "Vilambit") bandish*: A slow and steady melodic composition, usually in largo to adagio speeds
  + *[Madhyalaya](/wiki/Madhyalaya "Madhyalaya") bandish*: A medium tempo melodic composition, usually set in andante to allegretto speeds
  + *[Drut](/wiki/Drut "Drut") bandish*: A fast tempo melodic composition, usually set to allegretto speed or faster

Hindustani classical music is primarily vocal-centric, insofar as the musical forms were designed primarily for a vocal performance, and many instruments were designed and evaluated as to how well they emulate the human voice.

## Types of compositions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hindustani_classical_music&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Types of compositions")]

The major vocal forms or styles associated with Hindustani classical music are [dhrupad](/wiki/Dhrupad "Dhrupad"), [khyal](/wiki/Khyal "Khyal"), and [tarana](/wiki/Tarana "Tarana"). Light classical forms include [dhamar](/wiki/Dhamar_(music) "Dhamar (music)"), [trivat](/w/index.php?title=Trivat&action=edit&redlink=1 "Trivat (page does not exist)"), [chaiti](/wiki/Chaiti "Chaiti"), [kajari](/wiki/Kajari "Kajari"), [tappa](/wiki/Tappa "Tappa"), [tap-khyal](/w/index.php?title=Tap-khyal&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tap-khyal (page does not exist)"), [thumri](/wiki/Thumri "Thumri"), [dadra](/wiki/Dadra "Dadra"), [ghazal](/wiki/Ghazal "Ghazal") and [bhajan](/wiki/Bhajan "Bhajan"); these do not adhere to the rigorous rules of classical music.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

### Dhrupad

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hindustani_classical_music&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Dhrupad")]

Main article: [Dhrupad](/wiki/Dhrupad "Dhrupad")

Dhrupad is an old style of singing, traditionally performed by male singers. It is performed with a [tambura](/wiki/Tanpura "Tanpura") and a [pakhawaj](/wiki/Pakhavaj "Pakhavaj") as instrumental accompaniments. The lyrics, some of which were written in Sanskrit centuries ago, are presently often sung in [brajbhasha](/wiki/Brajbhasha "Brajbhasha"), a medieval form of North and East Indian languages that were spoken in Eastern India. The [rudra veena](/wiki/Rudra_veena "Rudra veena"), an ancient string instrument, is used in instrumental music in dhrupad.

Dhrupad music is primarily devotional in theme and content. It contains recitals in praise of particular deities. Dhrupad compositions begin with a relatively long and acyclic alap, where the syllables of the following mantra is recited:

> "*Om Anant tam Taran Tarini Twam Hari Om Narayan, Anant Hari Om Narayan*".

The alap gradually unfolds into more rhythmic jod and jhala sections. These sections are followed by a rendition of bandish, with the pakhawaj as an accompaniment. The great Indian musician [Tansen](/wiki/Tansen "Tansen") sang in the dhrupad style. A lighter form of dhrupad called [dhamar](/wiki/Dhamar_(music) "Dhamar (music)"), is sung primarily during the spring festival of [Holi](/wiki/Holi "Holi").

Dhrupad was the main form of northern Indian classical music until two centuries ago when it gave way to the somewhat less austere khyal, a more free-form style of singing. Since losing its main patrons among the royalty in Indian princely states, dhrupad risked becoming extinct in the first half of the twentieth century. However, the efforts by a few proponents, especially from the [Dagar](/wiki/Dagar_(disambiguation) "Dagar (disambiguation)") family, have led to its revival.

Some of the best known vocalists who sing in the Dhrupad style are the members of the Dagar lineage, including the senior Dagar brothers, [Nasir Moinuddin](/wiki/Nasir_Moinuddin_Dagar "Nasir Moinuddin Dagar") and [Nasir Aminuddin Dagar](/wiki/Nasir_Aminuddin_Dagar "Nasir Aminuddin Dagar"); the junior Dagar brothers, [Nasir Zahiruddin](/w/index.php?title=Nasir_Zahiruddin_Dagar&action=edit&redlink=1 "Nasir Zahiruddin Dagar (page does not exist)") and [Nasir Faiyazuddin Dagar](/w/index.php?title=Nasir_Faiyazuddin_Dagar&action=edit&redlink=1 "Nasir Faiyazuddin Dagar (page does not exist)"); and [Wasifuddin](/wiki/Wasifuddin_Dagar "Wasifuddin Dagar"), [Fariduddin](/w/index.php?title=Fariduddin_Dagar&action=edit&redlink=1 "Fariduddin Dagar (page does not exist)"), and [Sayeeduddin Dagar](/wiki/Sayeeduddin_Dagar "Sayeeduddin Dagar"). Other leading exponents include the [Gundecha Brothers](/wiki/Gundecha_Brothers "Gundecha Brothers") and [Uday Bhawalkar](/wiki/Uday_Bhawalkar "Uday Bhawalkar"), who have received training from some of the Dagars. Leading vocalists outside the Dagar lineage include the Mallik family of [Darbhanga tradition](/wiki/Darbhanga_gharana "Darbhanga gharana") of musicians; some of the leading exponents of this tradition were Ram Chatur Mallick, [Siyaram Tiwari](/wiki/Siyaram_Tiwari_(musician) "Siyaram Tiwari (musician)"), and Vidur Mallick. At present Prem Kumar Mallick, Prashant and Nishant Mallick are the Dhrupad vocalists of this tradition. A Very ancient 500 years old Dhrupad Gharana from Bihar is Dumraon Gharana, [Pt. Tilak Chand Dubey](/w/index.php?title=Pt._Tilak_Chand_Dubey&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pt. Tilak Chand Dubey (page does not exist)"), [Pt. Ghanarang Baba](/w/index.php?title=Pt._Ghanarang_Baba&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pt. Ghanarang Baba (page does not exist)") was founder of this prestigious Gharana. Dumraon Gharana [Dist-Buxar](/w/index.php?title=Dist-Buxar&action=edit&redlink=1 "Dist-Buxar (page does not exist)") is an ancient tradition of Dhrupad music nearly 500 years old. This Gharana flourished under the patronage of the king of Dumraon Raj. The dhrupad style (vanis) of the gharana is Gauhar, Khandar and Nauharvani. The living legends of this gharana is [Pt. Ramjee Mishra](/w/index.php?title=Pt._Ramjee_Mishra&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pt. Ramjee Mishra (page does not exist)").

A section of dhrupad singers of Delhi Gharana from [Mughal](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire") emperor [Shah Jahan](/wiki/Shah_Jahan "Shah Jahan")'s court migrated to [Bettiah](/wiki/Bettiah "Bettiah") under the patronage of the [Bettiah Raj](/wiki/Bettiah_Raj "Bettiah Raj"), giving rise to the Bettiah Gharana.[[11]](#cite_note-The_Times_of_India-11)

### Khyal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hindustani_classical_music&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Khyal")]

Main article: [Khyal](/wiki/Khyal "Khyal")

Khyal is the modern Hindustani form of vocal music. Khyal, literally meaning "thought" or "imagination" in [Hindustani](/wiki/Hindustani_language "Hindustani language") and derived from the Persian/Arabic term, is a two- to eight-line lyric set to a melody. Khyal contains a greater variety of embellishments and ornamentations compared to [dhrupad](/wiki/Dhrupad "Dhrupad"). Khyal's features such as *sargam* and *taan* as well as movements to incorporate dhrupad-style *alap* have led to it becoming popular.

The importance of the khyal's content is for the singer to depict, through music in the set [raga](/wiki/Raga "Raga"), the emotional significance of the khyal. The singer improvises and finds inspiration within the raga to depict the khyal.

The origin of Khyal is controversial, although it is accepted that this style was based on dhrupad and influenced by other musical traditions. Many argue that [Amir Khusrau](/wiki/Amir_Khusrau "Amir Khusrau") created the style in the late 14th-century. This form was popularized by Mughal emperor [Mohammad Shah](/wiki/Mohammad_Shah "Mohammad Shah") through his court musicians; some well-known composers of this period were [Sadarang](/wiki/Sadarang "Sadarang"), [Adarang](/wiki/Adarang "Adarang") and [Manrang](/w/index.php?title=Manrang&action=edit&redlink=1 "Manrang (page does not exist)").

### Tarana

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hindustani_classical_music&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Tarana")]

Main article: [Tarana](/wiki/Tarana "Tarana")

Another vocal form, taranas are medium- to fast-paced songs that are used to convey a mood of elation and are usually performed towards the end of a concert. They consist of a few lines of [bols](/wiki/Bol_(music) "Bol (music)") either from the rhythmic language of [Tabla](/wiki/Tabla "Tabla"), [Pakhawaj](/wiki/Pakhawaj "Pakhawaj") or [Kathak](/wiki/Kathak "Kathak") dance set to a tune. The singer uses these few lines as a basis for fast improvisation. The [tillana](/wiki/Tillana "Tillana") of Carnatic music is based on the tarana, although the former is primarily associated with dance.

### Tappa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hindustani_classical_music&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Tappa")]

Main article: [Tappa](/wiki/Tappa "Tappa")

Tappa is a form of Indian semi-classical vocal music whose specialty is its rolling pace based on fast, subtle, knotty construction. It originated from the folk songs of the camel riders of Punjab and was developed as a form of classical music by [Mian Ghulam Nabi Shori](/wiki/Mian_Ghulam_Nabi_Shori "Mian Ghulam Nabi Shori") or Shori Mian, a court singer for [Asaf-Ud-Dowlah](/wiki/Asaf-Ud-Dowlah "Asaf-Ud-Dowlah"), the [Nawab of Awadh](/wiki/Nawab_of_Awadh "Nawab of Awadh"). "Nidhubabur Tappa," or tappas sung by [Nidhu Babu](/wiki/Nidhu_Babu "Nidhu Babu") were very popular in 18th and 19th-century Bengal.

### Thumri

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hindustani_classical_music&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Thumri")]

Main article: [Thumri](/wiki/Thumri "Thumri")

Thumri is a semi-classical vocal form said to have begun in Uttar Pradesh with the court of [Nawab](/wiki/Nawab "Nawab") [Wajid Ali Shah](/wiki/Wajid_Ali_Shah "Wajid Ali Shah"), (r. 1847–1856). The lyrics are primarily in older, more rural Hindi dialects such as [Brij Bhasha](/wiki/Brij_Bhasha "Brij Bhasha"), [Awadhi](/wiki/Awadhi_language "Awadhi language"), and [Bhojpuri](/wiki/Bhojpuri_language "Bhojpuri language"). The themes covered are usually romantic in nature, hence giving more importance to lyrics rather than Raag, and bringing out the storytelling qualities of music. The need to express these strong emotional aesthetics makes Thumri and [Kathak](/wiki/Kathak "Kathak") a perfect match, which, before Thumri became a solo form, were performed together.

[Abdul Karim Khan](/wiki/Abdul_Karim_Khan "Abdul Karim Khan") performing a [Thumri](/wiki/Thumri "Thumri") in Raag [Bhairavi](/wiki/Bhairavi "Bhairavi")

Some recent performers of this genre are [Abdul Karim Khan](/wiki/Abdul_Karim_Khan "Abdul Karim Khan"), the brothers [Barkat Ali Khan](/wiki/Barkat_Ali_Khan "Barkat Ali Khan") and [Bade Ghulam Ali Khan](/wiki/Bade_Ghulam_Ali_Khan "Bade Ghulam Ali Khan"), [Begum Akhtar](/wiki/Begum_Akhtar "Begum Akhtar"), [Nirmala Devi](/wiki/Nirmala_Devi "Nirmala Devi"), [Girija Devi](/wiki/Girija_Devi "Girija Devi"), [Prabha Atre](/wiki/Prabha_Atre "Prabha Atre"), [Siddheshwari Devi](/wiki/Siddheshwari_Devi "Siddheshwari Devi"), [Shobha Gurtu](/wiki/Shobha_Gurtu "Shobha Gurtu") and [Chhannulal Mishra](/wiki/Chhannulal_Mishra "Chhannulal Mishra").

### Ghazal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hindustani_classical_music&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Ghazal")]

Main article: [Ghazal](/wiki/Ghazal "Ghazal")

In the Indian sub-continent during [Mughal](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire") rule, the Persian Ghazal became the most common poetic form in the Urdu language and was popularized by classical poets like [Mir Taqi Mir](/wiki/Mir_Taqi_Mir "Mir Taqi Mir"), [Ghalib](/wiki/Ghalib "Ghalib"), [Daagh](/wiki/Daagh "Daagh"), [Zauq](/wiki/Zauq "Zauq") and [Sauda](/wiki/Mirza_Muhammad_Rafi_Sauda "Mirza Muhammad Rafi Sauda") amongst the North Indian literary elite. The Ghazal genre is characterized by its romance, and its discourses on the various shades of love. Vocal music set to this mode of poetry is popular with multiple variations across [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia"), the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East"), as well as other countries and regions of the world.

## Instruments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hindustani_classical_music&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Instruments")]

Although Hindustani music clearly is focused on vocal performance, instrumental forms have existed since ancient times. In fact, in recent decades, especially outside South Asia, instrumental Hindustani music is more popular than vocal music, partly due to a somewhat different style and faster tempo, and partly because of a language barrier for the lyrics in vocal music.

Many musical instruments are associated with Hindustani classical music. The [veena](/wiki/Veena "Veena"), a string instrument, was traditionally regarded as the most important, but few play it today and it has largely been superseded by its cousins the [sitar](/wiki/Sitar "Sitar") and the [sarod](/wiki/Sarod "Sarod"), both of which owe their origin to Persian influences. The [tambura](/wiki/Tanpura "Tanpura") is also regarded as one of the most important instruments, due to its functioning as a fundamental layer that the rest of the instruments adhere to throughout a performance. Among bowed instruments, the [sarangi](/wiki/Sarangi "Sarangi") and [violin](/wiki/Violin "Violin") are popular. The [bansuri](/wiki/Bansuri "Bansuri"), [shehnai](/wiki/Shehnai "Shehnai") and [harmonium](/wiki/Pump_organ "Pump organ") are important wind instruments. In the percussion ensemble, the [tabla](/wiki/Tabla "Tabla") and the [pakhavaj](/wiki/Pakhavaj "Pakhavaj") are the most popular. Rarely used plucked or struck string instruments include the [surbahar](/wiki/Surbahar "Surbahar"), [sursringar](/wiki/Surasingar "Surasingar"), [santoor](/wiki/Santoor "Santoor"), and various versions of the [slide guitar](/wiki/Slide_guitar "Slide guitar"). Various other instruments have also been used in varying degrees.

## Festivals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hindustani_classical_music&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Festivals")]

One of the earliest modern music festivals focusing on Hindustani classical music was the [Harballabh Sangeet Sammelan](/wiki/Harballabh_Sangeet_Sammelan "Harballabh Sangeet Sammelan"), founded in 1875 in [Jallandhar](/wiki/Jallandhar "Jallandhar"). Others include [Sankatmochan Sangeet Samarth](/w/index.php?title=Sankatmochan_Sangeet_Samarth&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sankatmochan Sangeet Samarth (page does not exist)") in [Varanasi](/wiki/Varanasi "Varanasi"), [Dover Lane Music Conference](/wiki/Dover_Lane_Music_Conference "Dover Lane Music Conference") which notably debuted in 1952 in [Kolkata](/wiki/Kolkata "Kolkata"), [Sawai Gandharva Bhimsen Festival](/wiki/Sawai_Gandharva_Bhimsen_Festival "Sawai Gandharva Bhimsen Festival") in 1953 in [Pune](/wiki/Pune "Pune"), [ITC SRA Sangeet Sammelan](/wiki/ITC_SRA_Sangeet_Sammelan "ITC SRA Sangeet Sammelan") since the early 1970s, the Society for the Promotion of Indian Classical Music And Culture Amongst Youth or [SPIC MACAY](/wiki/SPIC_MACAY "SPIC MACAY") since 1977, [Pandit Nanhku Maharaj](/w/index.php?title=Pandit_Nanhku_Maharaj&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pandit Nanhku Maharaj (page does not exist)") since 1995.
